summarization.a: The subfigure shows the adsorption state of a diborane (B₂H₆) molecule on a Ti-terminated TiN(111) surface. Unlike on the (001) surface, the molecule interacts strongly with the Titanium atoms (yellow spheres).
summarization.b: The subfigure depicts the transition state where the B–B bond is elongated, facilitating the dissociation process.
summarization.c: The subfigure illustrates the reaction state (chemisorption), where the B₂H₆ has dissociated into two borane (BH₃) molecules. These BH₃ fragments are adsorbed onto the hollow sites formed by three Titanium atoms.

a.question: Describe the progression of the reaction mechanism depicted in subfigures (a) through (c).
a.answer: The figure illustrates the dissociative chemisorption of diborane. It begins with the adsorption of the B₂H₆ precursor on the Ti-rich surface (a), proceeds through a transition state characterized by B–B bond activation (b), and results in the cleavage of the molecule into two BH₃ fragments that chemically bond to the surface titanium atoms (c).

c.question: On the Ti-terminated TiN(111) surface shown in subfigure (c), to which specific geometric site do the dissociated BH₃ molecules adsorb?
c.answer: The dissociated BH₃ molecules adsorb onto the hollow sites formed by three Titanium (Ti) atoms (yellow spheres).

c.question: How does the adsorption site of the BH₃ fragments in this figure (Ti-terminated 111) differ from that observed in Figure 1 (TiN 001)?
c.answer: In Figure 1 on the TiN(001) surface, the BH₃ fragments bonded to the Nitrogen atoms. In contrast, on the Ti-terminated TiN(111) surface shown here, the BH₃ fragments preferentially bond to the Titanium atoms (specifically in the 3-fold hollow sites). This difference in binding site contributes to the different reactivities and energies observed between the two crystal orientations.

c.question: Is the adsorption of B₂H₆ on this Ti-terminated TiN(111) surface energetically favorable?
